title: Wintergreen Agent Prompt Pack v1
description: Production-tested prompts for building autonomous trading/research agents, orchestration loops, and quant workflows.

strategy_generation.title: Hypothesis-to-Backtest Bridge
strategy_generation.prompt: Given a trading hypothesis stated in plain English (e.g. 'BTC tends to mean-revert after a funding rate spike above 0.1%'), decompose it into: (1) a precise, falsifiable null hypothesis, (2) the exact entry/exit signal logic in pseudocode, (3) the minimum data fields required (OHLCV, funding, orderbook depth, etc.) and their required lookback window, (4) at least 2 confounding variables that could produce a false positive, and (5) the specific walk-forward validation split (train/test date ranges) that would falsify this hypothesis if it doesn't generalize. Do not write backtest code yet — output only the structured specification a backtesting engine could consume.

strategy_generation.title: Regime-Aware Signal Gate
strategy_generation.prompt: You are evaluating whether a trading signal should fire given the current market regime. Input: signal_name, signal_direction (long/short), current_regime (trending_up/trending_down/ranging/high_vol/low_vol), regime_confidence (0-1), and historical_win_rate_by_regime (a dict mapping regime name to win rate for this exact signal). Output a single JSON decision: {"fire": bool, "confidence_adjusted": float, "reasoning": str}. The signal should NOT fire if regime_confidence < 0.5, or if historical_win_rate_by_regime[current_regime] < 0.45. Explain your reasoning in one sentence referencing the specific regime and win rate that drove the decision.

strategy_generation.title: Kill Card Generator
strategy_generation.prompt: Write a kill card for a trading strategy candidate that failed validation. A kill card is a structured post-mortem with these exact fields: strategy_name, hypothesis_summary (1 sentence), kill_reason (one of: insufficient_edge, overfit_to_backtest_window, regime_dependent_only, transaction_costs_erase_edge, data_leakage_suspected, correlated_to_existing_signal), supporting_evidence (2-3 concrete numbers: sharpe, max_drawdown, win_rate, or p-value), and lessons_for_future_candidates (1-2 sentences of generalizable insight, not specific to this strategy). The tone should be blunt and evidence-based — no hedging language like 'might not' or 'could potentially'.

agent_orchestration.title: Maker-Checker Loop Exit Condition
agent_orchestration.prompt: Design the exit condition for an autonomous maker-checker loop where a 'maker' agent produces work and a separate 'checker' agent grades it. Given the task description below, output exactly: (1) the objective, non-gameable success criterion the checker uses (must be a concrete measurable check, e.g. 'all tests pass AND lint returns zero errors', not a subjective judgment), (2) a hard maximum iteration count and the reasoning for that number given the task's complexity, (3) a token/cost budget ceiling, (4) the specific condition under which the loop escalates to a human instead of continuing, and (5) one way a bad-faith maker could try to game the checker's criterion, and how the criterion is written to prevent that. Task: {{TASK_DESCRIPTION}}

agent_orchestration.title: Sub-Agent Task Decomposition
agent_orchestration.prompt: You are an orchestrator agent breaking a complex goal into parallel sub-agent tasks. Given the goal below, output a JSON array of sub-tasks where each entry has: task_id, goal (self-contained — the sub-agent has no memory of this conversation), context (all facts, file paths, and constraints the sub-agent needs, since it starts from zero), depends_on (list of task_ids that must complete first, or empty if independent), and estimated_complexity (low/medium/high). Group tasks that can run in parallel by giving them no dependencies on each other. Flag any task that touches a protected/irreversible action (spending money, sending external communication, modifying production credentials) with a "requires_human_approval": true field. Goal: {{GOAL_DESCRIPTION}}

agent_orchestration.title: Trace-to-Proposal Pattern Miner
agent_orchestration.prompt: Given a batch of execution traces (each trace has: task_type, decision, quality_signal, and outcome), identify recurring patterns that would justify a process improvement. Rules: (1) if fewer than 5 traces share a task_type, do NOT claim a statistical pattern — output only structural observations (missing fields, inconsistent formatting), (2) if 5+ traces exist for a task_type, compute the actual success rate and cite the exact count (e.g. '4 of 6 decisions with X property succeeded' — never round or estimate), (3) never propose a change to a protected surface (trading execution, credentials, public communication) without explicitly flagging it requires human approval, (4) output format is a structured proposal with evidence.trace_ids populated with the real trace IDs that support the claim — an empty evidence array invalidates the whole proposal.

market_analysis.title: Funding Rate Divergence Narrative
market_analysis.prompt: Given funding rate data for a perpetual futures pair across multiple exchanges (a list of {exchange, funding_rate, funding_interval_hours, open_interest}), write a 2-3 sentence trader-facing narrative explaining: (1) which exchange has the most extreme funding rate and in which direction, (2) whether this divergence is likely driven by open-interest imbalance or a temporary liquidity event, and (3) the most likely mean-reversion or continuation implication for the next funding period. Do not use hedge words like 'may' or 'could' more than once — state the most probable read directly, then note the confidence level as a single word (high/medium/low) at the end.

market_analysis.title: Liquidation Cluster Risk Read
market_analysis.prompt: Given a list of liquidation events (price, side, size_usd, timestamp) clustered near a specific price level, assess: (1) whether this cluster represents exhausted forced selling/buying (likely local reversal) or the start of a cascade (likely continuation), using the size and time-density of the cluster as your primary signal, (2) the nearest untested liquidity level above and below current price that could trigger the next cascade, and (3) a one-line risk note for a trader currently positioned in the direction of the cluster. Ground every claim in the specific numbers provided — do not generalize about 'liquidation clusters' as a category.
